speaker: interviewee
text: The way she survived--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was in the city of Tarnow. The ghetto finally was going to be made [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Her husband was a musician. And he taught some Polish girl. And they decided the Polish people were going to hide them, because the war wasn't going to last more than maybe three or four months. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they lived with them behind a wardrobe chest standing there all day, during the day and moving it out of the way to lie down at night, for two years and three months. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Quite a story, I mean. And they survived. And then she worked herself away from Poland through Krakow back to Germany. And then, she found me. And I went to live there. DP camp was some of the best days of my life. It was sort of living together in a camp. You knew everybody, lots of friends, lots of activities. The ORT, ORT is the-- O-R-T is a group that runs schools, training programs, American. And they came in and opened a bunch of schools. And I joined the radio school and began to study and trying to sort of recapture the lost time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then the possibility opened up for going to the States. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wasn't 18 yet, so I had the priority. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I didn't have to go in a quota. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As a matter of fact, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took all of the young people from Landsberg and other camps who were scheduled to go to America and took them to a very nice place on the lake, a children's camp. And to await there until the time that we're going to go to the States. Wonderful place, this was just great. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Unfortunately, they found the X-rays as part of my lungs. And they wouldn't let me in until they studied it for a while longer to see whether it's active or not. So in the meantime, I became older than 18 years. I had to leave the camp and went back to the-- to Landsberg. OK. In the meantime, my sister and her husband-- her husband was German, so their quota was little better than the Polish quota in terms of admission to this country. So they left for the States in February of 1947. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was on the hold with the X-ray. Now, if the X-ray was not the-- if I had something TB or anything like that, I wouldn't be allowed to enter the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, this was a time of some stress. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At any rate, I finally made it. The spot had not increased, so they let me in. And I came to this country in November of 1947. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I rejoined my sister. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had some form of a radio training, not very much from there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I started looking for jobs here, and I started working first of all in some radio assembly type places. It didn't work. 1947 was the-- television was becoming into the fore. And the radios died. There was no work. I was fired. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I worked for a while in a company. Oh, let me go back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My sister and I shared a room with a Jewish family in Brooklyn. They let us have a room. In the meantime, the-- my sister and her husband didn't get along. And they got divorced. So I was with my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had they been married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Married from 1936 until 1948 or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the only split up after.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: The war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the man of the house had a lifetime job in a company that made Venetian blinds and window shades. And he got me a job there as sort of a grease monkey working in a machine shop for two Germans who were Nazis through and through. [LAUGHS] So I worked there. But in the meantime, I always had the ability to read. I always loved to read. And I still could observe and learn from books. I learned to read English. And I started reading. And I was interested in hi-fi and electronics before long. I left the company, went to another company which was doing all kinds of electronic work for the government. And I guess within the year, I worked myself up to the-- to the project engineer working on the developed the first rotating head recorder for sounds. It was for the Navy. It was the forerunner of the video. Developed the first-- the forerunner of the cassette tapes. Actually, a narrow tape transistor was back in 1953. A few other things-- I never went to school. I joined the National Guard in 1948, because the draft law was going into effect. And there was-- I knew we [INAUDIBLE]. I was in this country only a six months. I read from deck to deck everyday or pretty much deck to deck. And I learned about the draft law being passed. And if I signed up with the National Guard before the law was signed, I would be considered to be in the military, therefore not subject to draft. So I started there. I advanced there as well, as well. I mean, intellectually, I was doing excellent. My mind was working perfect. But below my neck, it wasn't so good. Except, I didn't know. I didn't know yet what is normal or not normal. I was functioning in every way, having fun, doing things, making progress.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't know if anything was missing. I had to compare it to something. Things were missing right from the beginning, but just I didn't know that. Then I met this girl, Marion Dreifuss. And we got married in 1953. How did you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: We met through the-- her brother, who worked in the same company. So he arranged for a blind date with her. She was German. In the 1930's-- you may know about this-- that England arranged to allow a number of Jewish children from Germany to go to England and to be taken into various foster homes as a means or ways of escaping from Germany. And the Germans let the kids go. So she was taken from Germany, stayed with a family in Coventry. She was bombed twice. She-- her life was saved, because the ceiling collapsed as a bomb hit the house. Because she had a dog, a German shepherd, who just before the ceiling collapsed, jumped on top of her. And it was killed, but saved her. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got married in '53. She became pregnant in 1954. And within a few weeks of the-- we knew about the pregnancy, she developed a-- a swelling in her neck, went to the doctor found there was Hodgkin's Disease. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: She gave birth [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to a daughter in '55 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in May and never left the hospital. The disease progressed worse and worse. And finally, she died the following year at 23 years of age. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I couldn't take the-- my daughter, I didn't know what to do with her. I was working. I had nobody to take care of her. I put her in orphanage, who worked on me continuous to give her away for adoption. Says, you don't want that. Give her away, give her away. I couldn't do that. In the meantime, my sister remarried. She lived in Denver, Colorado, had a nice life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she had-- couldn't have any children. So she says, I'll take her for you. As it turned out later on, she was growing up. And she-- she was a Kempler. And she was living with some-- you know. I was told and recommend, give her away for adoption to you sister, which I did. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So this sister-- this-- the-- I developed, among my mental quirks-- not only the running away as I did from my parents. But I began to run away from people before I got too close to them. Because every time, love and death to me became synonymous. Whenever I loved somebody, they died. So it became bad words. My vocabulary love is bad word, mother a bad word. They all produced reaction, which I didn't know how to cope with.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I began to isolate myself from people. I liked my books. I liked my music. I could sit and listen to music. Or, I had the best hi-fi going and stuff like that and work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is-- was my life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then after she died, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I switch companies. I went to work for a company in Connecticut, the magnetic tape. So I used to commute from New York to Connecticut for a while. And the job was ideal. There's all kinds of opportunity, all kinds of growth. And I-- I did very well, as usual, by-- this was sufficiently new field. There was-- there was-- no-- there's no place-- you can't learn about in school, anyplace else. So I was always ahead of everybody else, because I had the books nobody else wanted to read.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I always knew a little bit-- as little as I knew, I always knew a little bit more than the next guy. So this worked very well. On a personal level, I was-- I was stuck. I lived with a lady I describe as living in a box. Because my own reality was that of a [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] intellectual-- let's put it this way. How do I describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Intellectually, I was functioning very well. So from all outward appearancing, I was as normal as can be. Emotionally, I was totally stuck and frozen in place. Frozen would be a good way to describe it, or paralyzed, or even dead. But I didn't know that. I just knew something was wrong. But I knew I would not-- I would gradually over the time give up friends, not pursue writing letters or keeping in touch, or making friends. I would give it up. I would run away from anything that was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] too close to commitment or taking responsibility for others for their well-being. It became very scary type of reactions. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What came later on, which was really a big change in my life, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got in touch with Jehovah's Witnesses accidentally. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, these people impressed me very much during the war as I told you. As a matter of fact-- this is a little aside story. While I was in Landsberg, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they conducted-- the American military conducted a trial against guards of Mauthausen camps. But this was not the big shots, but the guards and people against whom there was no evidence. They had locked-- locked them up in Dachau, form a concentration camp. And now, they were looking for people who were in these camps who possibly can present evidence which can be used in the trials, just pre-trial testimony and so on. So, I was called there. I spent three weeks there. And, again, I ran into two witnesses who were there in the camps. This is the first time I talked to them. One was paralyzed in the right arm. Because what they did to him trying to break him, is to hang him backwards like this, on poles was bolts between the fingers and just hanging like this. Tied them up from the top, hanging like this for eight hours. What they-- what they used to do in this pre-trial thing is to bring the Germans one after another on the stage. And then two Americans would interview them. And then anybody who had anything to offer, they would then testify, and then prepare a written statement.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one guy gets up on the stage. And this-- I was sitting next to the witnesses. I liked them, they were sort of kind, nice people. He puffs his pipe. And he nudges me and says, oh, boy. This is the guy that did it to me. I said, are you going to testify? He says, no. I says, why? Because I am-- the vengeance belongs to God. I said, are you crazy? What kind of a nut are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is your opportunity. I was furious with him. He wouldn't budge. I mean, this is the kind of people they were. So I had the admiration for them even though I knew zero about them. So when I ran into them, religion to me was the last thing in my mind. Because I mean, when I was a kid in school and when I studied the Torah, the Mishnah, Gemara, and Hebrew, I mean, down to this day at this-- things-- I forget things today. But I have a very good memory recollection of what I studied then. I can still-- almost fluent in these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had a very good understanding of the Jewish religion and traditions, all the way to the Kabbalah for that matter. But during the war, the idea of God, it sort of-- I had no use for God. Because if-- who needs a God? If you-- if you were in a camp, who needs him? What did he do for me? Where was He? So I became not only an atheist, I would call myself almost a God hater. So somebody comes to me and talks religion, especially something to do with Christianity, forget it. I wasn't interested in that. But I would talk to them, because I wanted to hear what they have to say. What made them different? I wanted to know why they behaved like this. What do they believe? What made them stand out under these conditions? And I began to talk to them. And what I hear appealed to my intellect. It made sense. It-- the-- the-- everything was falling into place in terms of what this is all about? Why did this happen? What is-- why-- where are we going? What is the purpose of man? The whole thing began to make sense to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And eventually, I said, I want to be one of you. And I did starting back in 1958. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I had a hard time with it. Because this religion, unlike other religions, is built on love and commitment and devotion, precisely the kind of things that I did not feel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was very good at understanding at analyzing and teaching. Again, the head part was there. But there was nothing in the heart. But I didn't know that. Being dead or paralyzed emotionally, you don't know that you're dead unless something happens.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I met my wife. She was one of the Jehovah's Witnesses, Virginia. We married in 1963. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And now, she is totally spirited, wonderful, normal All-American girl full of verve and energy and life. And I talked to her before that. I was all kinds of fears. The idea of marriage was scary to me, but I didn't know why. And she would listen for hours. And we decided to get married. But then the fact is that I was only functioning partially. Emotionally, I was still living in a box. Even though we were married, I wasn't fully here. She-- I denied it. I didn't realize. She was very unhappy. I was self-center-- I was doing everything I was supposed to. But not-- I wasn't here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When the children were born, two fine boys, wonderful boys. They didn't have a father really. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because I was-- I was living almost in a state of panic of responsibility for others. I could not take the responsibility for others. And so it became-- this was a very difficult time. Because I knew I was causing pain to them, to my wife, and then to my children, and my daughter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She couldn't understand. I mean, she knew I gave her away. She didn't quite understand the whole thing. In the meantime, my sister's husband committed suicide. I mean, he was full of verve and life and a wonderful guy. He committed suicide. I still don't know the reason why. My daughter discovered him hanging in the basement. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My sister never recovered from the situation emotionally. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I couldn't support anybody [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] emotionally. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: My children-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean one-- the older one is not vocal, but he has suffered. The younger one is very vocal and says, why didn't you do anything for us, dad? I mean, you never played games with me. You never took me here, never took me there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I cringed. I had no answers. When they were growing up, sometimes they were saying, Earth calling Dad. You know, a zombie. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I caused a great deal of hurt and pain without wanting to cause hurt and pain. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't quite do anything about this, since I did not understand what was going on. To my own mind, I was-- everything I was doing OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was an OK guy, but I wasn't. And the same goes for my association with Jehovah's Witnesses. I could not quite-- it wasn't in my heart to do these things. I would do things. But everything became-- everything I did in life was a question of duty and responsibility. And this was the only motivation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it-- it's-- this continued for a long time. It's only recently [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I began to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] make progress. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And finally, I left my box. Even though for some years, I would sort of go out into the real world, but I always came back. Finally, I went back, and I stopped running. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So at this point, I am out of it. I'm making wonderful progress in terms of the-- to see the reality, to try to fix things, try to be different in my family. Hopefully, it's not too late.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife is discouraged, because she has seen me come out before, then I went back again. Now, at this point, I'm convinced. Because it's been holding for a long time. And all of the things which are associated with living in the box and running, all the symptoms of this are gone. The primary reason why this is working now-- I mean, I don't know. There are several things. For one, thing it's a question of the time. I have retired last year. Work was very important to me. It was my source of my self-esteem, source of my self-worth and all that. And when I retired, I thought I couldn't live without it. I found out not only I can do very well without it. But I began to devote a lot of my time, most of my time to working on myself, the true-- my identity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do I come from? What is this emotional death? And how do I get back to life? And part of it was to my activity with Jehovah's Witnesses. This is became now something which has opened up my heart. It has a purpose and meaning. So this is a mutual. One hand-- one thinks the same before as one-- that the marriage and the children and the religious activity were all destructive. One fed on another in a negative way. Now they're feeding on one another in a very positive way. So I'm like a new person. I feel-- I mean the-- the stress that I was living under all the time is almost all gone. I feel free. I mean, it's-- it's a lot to say about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not necessarily for now. But I began to grow up. For the past 50 odd years, I consider myself to be 14. I never moved an inch beyond 14 emotionally. Now, this is as real a statement as can be made. And I not only be-- I acted. I had some bizarre behavior, which was maybe normal for a 14 year old, but not for 30, 40, or 50 or 60 year old. But I was stuck in one place emotionally. I'm beginning to grow up. And it's a wonderful feeling. But this type of child that I was-- I mean, I was an adult, too. And people saw me as an adult. But inside, I saw myself only one way, as the 14 year old. And I can trace it right back to the day in the meadow where something broke. Some of the things that caused me problems were many survivors suffer guilt for one reason or another.

speaker: interviewer
text: I felt that I condemned my mother to death. And I punished her for betraying me, even though she saved my life with the policeman. So this was a case of a Sophie's Choice, too. Except this was a choice that I made voluntarily on my own, not like she was forced to make the choice. Are you familiar with the story of Sophie's Choice?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was forced to make a decision. I wasn't. So I've suffered guilt all these years, because what I have done. And even though she didn't die at that time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the-- the fact that I ran away to survive, I did the right thing. If I stayed with them, all of us would be dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't feel bad about it. But my feeling trace back to the days in-- at that day in the meadow, and then in the forest. So they're not-- they're not logical. Guilt is something-- the guilt and then, of course, the fear and the panic, the idea of-- of running away from love or running away from people, because of the sense of loss was the only motivator which made me act.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a fear of loss. Something was impending. And I could-- by taking some action, I can [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] prevent that loss. Then I would act with my full energies and abilities. As soon as the loss of the threat was gone, I sank back into the box. No matter how hard I tried, I couldn't prevent it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So this is what was happening all these years. And finally, it's coming around. And I hope it's going to stay. I feel like a totally new person. And almost daily, there are new insights, new awarenesses and things which make me freer and freer and freer. And I can now see into what was happening. I see myself as a-- as a person for the first time.

speaker: interviewer
text: The story that you've told today, is that something that you've shared often with family, your children, grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, grandchildren I never talk to them about it. They are too young. I have talked to my children up to a point. As a matter of fact, part of my growing up is my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] desire to get closer to the children. I still find it difficult. I talk to them, but the talking is still a little bit superficial. They do not understand yet. And I'm not quite ready to-- this is still in the process.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody has heard part of it. Not everybody in the family has heard all of it that I told you. And there is far, far more of that nobody knows. So this is still in the process. The thing is, I am always have been [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what's the word? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm a survivor. I never give up. And I can be fall down, and I picked myself up again and again and again. That's why at this age I've never said to myself, I'm too old to change.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the way I am. If I'm not satisfied with the way I am, I keep fighting and fighting and fighting. And finally, I see the results.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- I feel that the heart is opening up, which was totally blocked. And it's a great feeling. And it's something I'm very happy about, especially since the progress is truly manifested on a daily basis. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that's-- Do you have any--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] stop.
